content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
  
 
April 14, 2020 
 
Robert Khuu 
Aleshire & Wynder, LLP 
Deputy City Attorney 
City of Signal Hill 
18881 Von Karman Ave., Suite 1700 
Irvine, CA 92612 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No.  A-20-052 
 
Dear Mr. Khuu: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1   
 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions change, then you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
 
QUESTIONS 
 
1. Under the Act, may Signal Hill City Councilmember Edward Wilson take part in City 
Council decision to potentially contract with a consultant to study the costs and benefits 
of the City authorizing commercial marijuana activities? 
2. May Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions to amend the City’s  
Municipal Code to authorize, or to continue to ban, commercial marijuana activities in 
the City? 
3. If the City Council decides to amend the Municipal Code to allow commercial marijuana 
activities, may Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions to adopt any 
regulatory, development, or services fee related to the City’s adopted commercial 
marijuana activity regulations? 
 
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-20-052 
Page No. 2 
 
 
4. May Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions on whether to submit a 
tax upon commercial marijuana activities within the City to the City’s electorate? 
 
CONCLUSION 
 
1. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in the City Council’s decision to contract 
with a consultant because it would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
Councilmember Wilson’s economic interests. 
2. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in decisions to amend the Municipal Code 
and authorize commercial marijuana activities, as such decisions would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on his source of income, the Marijuana 
Company. Specifically, the decisions would not result in a change of at least five percent 
of the Marijuana Company’s annual gross revenues. 
3. Councilmember may not take part in any decision to adopt any regulatory, development, 
or services fees related to the City’s adopted commercial marijuana activity regulations 
that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the Marijuana 
Company.  
4. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in a decision to submit a tax upon 
commercial marijuana activities within the City to the City’s electorate, as that decision 
would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect upon the Marijuana 
Company, given that it does not appear the revenue subject to such a tax would meet the 
materiality threshold of at least $250,000 in additional expenses. 
 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
The City of Signal Hill (“City”) is located within the City of Long Beach. In 2019, Signal 
Hill’s population was estimated as 11,795 residents. Except as required by state law, the City 
currently has a complete ban on all commercial marijuana activities within the City. The City’s 
Municipal Code defines “commercial marijuana activity” as “the cultivation, possession, 
manufacture, distribution, processing, storing, laboratory testing, packaging, labeling, 
transportation, delivery, or sale of marijuana and marijuana products; but not including personal 
marijuana cultivation in compliance with Chapter 9.50 (Personal Marijuana Cultivation) of this 
code.” 
 
 
The City Council will be considering whether to lift the ban on any of the enumerated 
commercial marijuana activities in the City. It may ultimately decide to authorize none, some, or all 
of the enumerated commercial marijuana activities described in the above-mentioned definition. 
The City Council will contract with a consultant to conduct an analysis of the costs and benefits to 
the City if any commercial marijuana activities are authorized. In order to authorize any commercial 
marijuana activities in the City, the City Council must amend the City’s Municipal Code, including 
the City’s Zoning Code and any adopted specific plans (“Code”). It would also have to study and 
adopt related development, regulatory, and/or service fees. 
 
 
If the City Council decides to authorize commercial marijuana activities, then it would also 
have to consider whether to contract with a consultant to operate any City program to implement 
the City’s adopted commercial marijuana activity regulations. If the City Council decides to 


File No. A-20-052 
Page No. 3 
 
 
authorize commercial marijuana activities, then it would also have to consider whether to submit a 
tax on such commercial marijuana activities to a vote by the City’s electorate. 
 
 
City Councilmember Edward H.J. Wilson is currently employed by a company that 
cultivates marijuana (“Marijuana Company”). He has no ownership or property interests in the 
Marijuana Company. The Marijuana Company has a cultivation facility in the City of Long Beach 
adjacent to the City’s limits. The facility has over 100,000 square feet and is located on a parcel that 
would allow for future expansion. If commercial marijuana activities are authorized, there is a 
possibility that the Marijuana Company will do any of the following: 
 
(1) Sell its cultivated marijuana and marijuana products to marijuana dispensaries located 
within the City. 
(2) Expand into other types of commercial marijuana activities within the City. 
(3) Open another facility within the City for the purpose of operating any of the commercial 
marijuana activities. 
 
In a follow-up email, you clarified that the Marijuana Company has been in operation for 
approximately one year, with annual revenue of approximately $25,000,000. The company 
currently operates on a state-wide level and potential expansion into the Signal Hill market would 
be small and likely represent an increase in annual revenue of less than 1%. 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
“No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in 
making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.” (Section 87100.) “A public 
official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,” or on certain 
enumerated economic interests including “[a]ny source of income . . . aggregating five hundred 
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made.” (Section 87103(c).) 
 
 
Based on the facts provided, Councilmember Wilson does not have an economic interest 
explicitly involved in the governmental decisions at issue. Regulation 18701(b) provides the 
standard for determining whether a decision’s financial effect on an economic interest not explicitly 
involved in the decision is reasonably foreseeable. It states in relevant part: “[a] financial effect 
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official’s control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.” 
 
 
When a governmental decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a 
public official’s source of income, and that source of income is a business entity, Regulation 
18702.1 provides the relevant standards for determining whether that financial effect is material. 
(See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Regulation 18702.1 provides that such a financial effect is material 
where the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity’s annual gross revenues, or 


File No. A-20-052 
Page No. 4 
 
 
the value of the entity’s assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or greater than $1,000,000 or five 
percent of the entity’s annual gross revenues and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000. 
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).) Similarly, such an effect is material where the decision may cause the 
entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to 
or more than $250,000 or one percent of the entity’s annual gross revenues and the change in 
expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).) 
 
 
The legalization of certain commercial marijuana activities would potentially increase the 
Marijuana Company’s annual gross revenues. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).) You have noted that the 
Marijuana Company has an annual revenue of approximately $25,000,000, and expansion into 
Signal Hill would likely represent an increase of less than one percent in annual revenue. 
Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) requires that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect equal either 
$1,000,000 or five percent of the entity’s annual gross revenues and at least $10,000.  Based upon 
the facts provided, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the legalization of commercial marijuana 
activities in Signal Hill could increase the Marijuana Company’s annual gross revenues by the 
applicable materiality standard. This is supported by the fact that the Marijuana Company is a state-
wide business and is unlikely to be materially affected by sales in Signal Hill, which has a 
population of fewer than 12,000. Thus, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect that legalizing 
commercial marijuana activities would have on the Marijuana Company is not material. 
 
The Act also permits Councilmember Wilson to take part in the City Council’s potential 
submission of a proposed tax on commercial marijuana activities. Although any potentially 
foreseeable financial effect would ultimately depend on the voters of Signal Hill approving such a 
tax, any such financial effect upon the Marijuana Company would not be material. As previously 
discussed, the Marijuana Company has annual revenue of approximately $25,000,000. The 
imposition of a tax on commercial marijuana activities would constitute an additional expense for 
the Marijuana Company, but an additional expense is only considered material if it would be equal 
to or greater than $250,000 or one percent of the entity’s annual gross revenues and the change in 
expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).) Here, the entire estimated increase in the 
Marijuana Company’s revenue as a result of doing business in Signal Hill is less than one percent, 
or $250,000. Inherently, then, a tax on commercial marijuana activities in Signal Hill could not 
meet the applicable materiality standard under Regulation 18702.1(a)(3). Therefore, 
Councilmember Wilson is not prohibited from taking part in such a decision.2 
 
 
With respect to contracting with a consultant, any financial effect that such a decision would 
have upon the Marijuana Company is entirely hypothetical and contingent upon subsequent 
governmental decisions. The mere act of authorizing a study does not impart any financial effect on 
marijuana-related businesses. Therefore, the City Council’s decision of whether to contract with a 
consultant would have no reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon the Marijuana Company 
 
 
Finally, you have also inquired as to whether Councilmember Wilson may take part in City 
Council decisions to adopt any regulatory, development, or services fees related to the City’s 
adopted commercial marijuana activity regulations. Without details regarding specific 
 
2 We note, however, that if the tax was not upon commercial marijuana activities themselves, such as business 
transactions, but rather on real property owned by the Marijuana Company, a different materiality standard would likely 
be applicable and prohibit Councilmember Wilson from taking part in the decision. (See Regulations 18702.1(a)(4), 
18702.2(a)(3).) 


File No. A-20-052 
Page No. 5 
 
 
governmental decisions, we cannot determine whether any such decision would have a reasonably 
foreseeable, material financial effect on Councilmember Wilson’s economic interests, including his 
source of income interest in the Marijuana Company. The above analysis of a decision’s financial 
effect on the Marijuana Company does not include all the standards under which a material 
financial effect may be found. Other standards may be relevant depending on the details and 
circumstances of the governmental decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Wilson should seek 
additional advice if he is faced with a specific governmental decision for which he is able to provide 
sufficient details, and he is uncertain about whether the Act permits or prohibits his taking part in 
the decision. 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
Dave Bainbridge 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Kevin Cornwall 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division 
 
KMC:aja

QUESTION: 1. Under the Act, may Signal Hill City Councilmember Edward Wilson take part in City 
Council decision to potentially contract with a consultant to study the costs and benefits 
of the City authorizing commercial marijuana activities? 
2. May Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions to amend the City’s  
Municipal Code to authorize, or to continue to ban, commercial marijuana activities in 
the City? 
3. If the City Council decides to amend the Municipal Code to allow commercial marijuana 
activities, may Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions to adopt any 
regulatory, development, or services fee related to the City’s adopted commercial 
marijuana activity regulations?4. May Councilmember Wilson take part in City Council decisions on whether to submit a 
tax upon commercial marijuana activities within the City to the City’s electorate?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in the City Council’s decision to contract 
with a consultant because it would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
Councilmember Wilson’s economic interests. 
2. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in decisions to amend the Municipal Code 
and authorize commercial marijuana activities, as such decisions would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on his source of income, the Marijuana 
Company. Specifically, the decisions would not result in a change of at least five percent 
of the Marijuana Company’s annual gross revenues. 
3. Councilmember may not take part in any decision to adopt any regulatory, development, 
or services fees related to the City’s adopted commercial marijuana activity regulations 
that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the Marijuana 
Company.  
4. Yes, Councilmember Wilson may take part in a decision to submit a tax upon 
commercial marijuana activities within the City to the City’s electorate, as that decision 
would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect upon the Marijuana 
Company, given that it does not appear the revenue subject to such a tax would meet the 
materiality threshold of at least $250,000 in additional expenses.
